queue.goal: Upgrade monkey-lang GC from mark-sweep to generational: nursery (young gen) with fast bump allocation, write barrier for old→young references, minor collection (scavenge nursery), major collection (full mark-sweep), promotion to old gen after surviving N collections

queue.summary: 10 complex integration tests: GCD, sum 1..100, power, prime sieve, Collatz, fib sequence, nested calls. 51 total tests all pass. End-to-end: monkey source → parse → codegen → assemble → RISC-V execute.

queue.task: Depth check: 5 BUILDs done (T83 IC, T84 IC bench, T89 RISC-V codegen, T91 integration tests, plus T90 skip). All surprised me (stack frame bug, IC 100% hit rate). Direction for next 4 hours?
queue.summary: Depth check 3/3 YES. Not breadth spiraling. Direction: RISC-V register allocation (linear scan or callee-saved registers). Currently all values go through stack — real optimization opportunity.

queue.goal: RISC-V register allocator: (1) peephole pass to eliminate redundant load/stores, (2) map frequently-used locals to callee-saved registers (s1-s11), (3) measure cycle count improvement vs stack-only
queue.summary: PLAN: 2 BUILD tasks — peephole optimizer + register allocator for RISC-V backend.

queue.task: Peephole optimizer for RISC-V assembly: eliminate redundant push/pop pairs (sw+lw same reg same offset), merge consecutive addi to sp, remove dead mv a0 a0. Run as post-pass on assembly text. Benchmark before/after cycle counts.
queue.summary: Peephole optimizer: 5 patterns (self-move, push-pop-same, push-pop-mv, merge-addi-sp, store-load-elim). 15.2% cycle reduction on fib(10). 26 tests all pass.

queue.task: Register allocator: map let-bound variables to callee-saved registers s1-s11 (instead of stack slots). Track live ranges, spill to stack when registers exhausted. Save/restore used callee-saved regs in prologue/epilogue. Benchmark fib(10) and prime sieve cycle counts.
queue.summary: Register allocator: map locals to callee-saved s1-s11, spill to stack. Combined with peephole: fib 6% faster, fact 6% faster. 33 tests all pass. Learned: recursive calls make register save/restore overhead significant.

queue.task: Next depth on RISC-V backend: Run compiled code through the pipeline simulator (5-stage, branch predictor, caches). This would benchmark the compiled code realistically. Also: string support (data section), or connecting to the existing playground.
queue.summary: Direction: Run compiled monkey code through 5-stage pipeline + Tomasulo OOO. Get IPC, hazard stats, pipeline diagram. Connects codegen → pipeline → performance analysis.

queue.task: Pipeline integration: compile monkey-lang programs, run through PipelineCPU and TomasuloCPU. Compare IPC, stall cycles, forwarding stats. Show how register allocation affects pipeline efficiency. Tests + benchmark report.
queue.summary: Pipeline integration: run compiled monkey through 5-stage PipelineCPU. Register mode IPC=0.97 vs stack IPC=0.90. Register mode eliminates load-use stalls. Recursive programs hit memory bounds in pipeline sim. 8 tests.

queue.task: 10+ BUILDs cumulative today. Depth check: learned stack frame layout, IC hit rates, pipeline IPC, register allocation tradeoffs. What's genuinely new territory? Consider: branch predictor optimization for compiled code, or switch to stress-testing existing infrastructure.
queue.summary: Depth check 3/3 YES. Direction: stress-test the RISC-V codegen. Compile increasingly complex/adversarial monkey programs. Find bugs in edge cases: deeply nested expressions, many variables (spill test), large loops, etc.

queue.task: Stress-test RISC-V codegen: edge cases (deeply nested exprs, 20+ variables forcing register spill, mutual recursion, large while loops, negative numbers, overflow, if-chains with 10+ branches). Fix any bugs found.
queue.summary: Stress testing found 1 bug: large integer encoding (lui vs li). Fixed and added 84 stress tests covering edge cases (spill, deep nesting, negatives, large numbers, Ackermann). 202 total RISC-V pipeline tests all pass.

queue.task: What is the hardest unsolved problem in monkey-lang? RISC-V backend is solid. Consider: array/string support in RISC-V codegen (requires heap allocator in assembly), or type inference integration with existing type-inference project, or improving the JIT with IC knowledge.
queue.summary: Direction: heap allocator in RISC-V assembly. Required for array/string support in compiled code. Implement bump allocator with arena, then compile monkey-lang arrays to use heap allocation. Genuinely hard systems work.

queue.goal: Heap allocator for RISC-V compiled monkey-lang: (1) bump allocator in assembly (heap region in high memory, allocation pointer, bump on alloc), (2) array representation (header: length + capacity, followed by element words), (3) compile array literals and array indexing

queue.task: Implement bump allocator for RISC-V: initialize heap pointer (gp register), _alloc subroutine (takes size in a0, returns pointer in a0), integrate with codegen prologue. Test: allocate and write to heap memory.
Compile array literals and indexing: ArrayLiteral → allocate header+elements on heap, store length+values. IndexExpression on arrays → compute offset, load element. Support len() builtin. 15+ tests.
